title: On the Riemann problem for the Adlam-Allen model
authors: Su Yang, Marco Calabrese, Vassilis Koukouloyannis, Panayotis G. Kevrekidis
abstract: In the present work, we revisit the Adlam-Allen (AA) model in order to investigate its numerically observed rarefaction and dispersive shock waves that arise in numerical simulations of the Riemann problem associated with the model. On the one hand, we perform a direct analysis of the rarefaction and dispersive shock waves of the AA model via examining its corresponding dispersionless system and leveraging the DSW-fitting method to obtain theoretical predictions on various edge features of the dispersive shock waves. On the other hand, we review the KdV reduction of the AA model and utilize the KdV dispersive shock wave to approximate that of the AA model. Relevant numerical comparisons demonstrate the good performance of not only the direct analysis on the AA dispersive shock wave, but also of the approximation via the KdV DSW. These methodologies provide a systematic toolbox for analyzing the outcome of Riemann problems in not only this fundamental setting of cold plasmas but also potentially in related plasma-physics problems.
summary: This paper provides a systematic toolkit for analyzing shock wave dynamics in the Adlam-Allen model, a fundamental model for plasma physics. By using Whitham modulation theory and KdV reduction, the authors successfully predict the complex structures of dispersive shock waves and rarefaction waves. The results show that even simple asymptotic reductions can accurately capture the behavior of more complex nonlinear plasma waves.
main_problem: The study investigates the formation and structure of rarefaction and dispersive shock waves (DSWs) in the Adlam-Allen model, specifically addressing the Riemann problem with jump initial data.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the DSW-fitting method and the KdV reduction provide highly accurate theoretical predictions for the edge features and amplitudes of shock waves in the Adlam-Allen model.
method: The research employs Whitham modulation theory, the DSW-fitting method, and a KdV asymptotic reduction, validated against numerical simulations using RK4 and pseudospectral methods.
model_or_system: The Adlam-Allen model is a system of nonlinear partial differential equations describing hydromagnetic waves in cold, collisionless, magnetospheric plasmas.
key_observables: Dispersive shock wave (DSW) amplitudes, edge speeds (solitonic and linear), wavenumber, and rarefaction wave profiles.
important_parameters: The non-dissipative regime, the dispersionless limit, the small-amplitude/long-wavelength (KdV) limit, and the carrier density/magnetic field constants (R0, B0).
main_assumptions: The KdV reduction is an approximation valid primarily for small-amplitude jumps, and the analysis focuses on the non-dissipative, one-dimensional case.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows permitted parameter regions for traveling waves; Figure 2 shows space-time evolution of a DSW; Figure 3 shows rarefaction wave profiles; Figure 4 and 5 compare theoretical predictions for DSW edges against numerical data; Figure 6 compares the AA model directly to the KdV reduction.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the definition of the Adlam-Allen model and its physical context, followed by the derivation of traveling wave solutions and modulation equations, then moves to the analysis of the dispersionless limit and the DSW-fitting method, and concludes with a comparison between the full model and its KdV reduction via numerical validation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Permitted (light-colored) and non-permitted (dark-colored) regions in the (E,M)-parametric space, for three choices of the speed, namely c = 1,1.5,2.

Figure 2: The dispersive shock wave of the AA model (1.1). The panel (a) depicts the space-time evolution of the DSW, where the two dashed blue lines represent the theoretical predictions on the linear and solitonic edges of the DSW based on the DSW-fitting results. (b) shows the spatial profile of the DSW at t = 200 with background value w−= 0.4.

Figure 3: The dynamics of the numerical rarefaction waves of the AA model (1.1). The panel (a) depicts the com- parison of the self-similar solution (6.3) (in dashed red) with the numerically observed rarefaction wave (in solid blue) at t = 200. Note that w−= 0 and w+ = 0.1. The panel (b) displays the spatio-temporal evolution dynamics of the numerical rarefaction wave of the AA model in Eq. (1.1).

Figure 4: The comparison of the solitonic-edge DSW features. The panel (a) depicts the comparison of the solitonic-edge amplitudes of the DSW while (b) shows that of the solitonic-edge speeds. The blue curves and dis- crete red squares represent the DSW-fitting theoretical predictions and the numerically estimated counterparts, respectively. Note also that w+ is always fixed to be zero, while the values of w−are varied within the interval of [0.1,0.5].
Figure 5: The comparison of the linear-edge DSW speeds. The blue curve and red squares refer to the DSW-fitting theoretical prediction and the numerically computed counterparts, respectively.
